1731	
1732	The ambient finiteness transfer (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer})
1733	factors the polynomial action of the larger ring through a surjection onto the
1734	smaller one. The following blocks build that surjection and the semilinearity
1735	identity it rests on, and record the two elementary finiteness manipulations
1736	(shrinking a numerator, enlarging a denominator) used to feed the kernel and
1737	cokernel constructors.

2933	\begin{lemma}[Flat base change preserves finite equalizers]
2934	  \label{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
2935	  \lean{LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv}
2936	  \mathlibok
2937	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
2938	  Let \(A \to B\) be a flat ring homomorphism (more generally, let \(B\) be a flat
2939	  \(A\)-module that is also an \(A\)-algebra, with the scalar-tower compatibility),
2940	  and let \(f, g : N \rightrightarrows P\) be a pair of \(A\)-linear maps. Write
2941	  \(\operatorname{eq}(f, g) = \{x \in N : f(x) = g(x)\}\) for their equalizer. Then the
2942	  canonical \(B\)-linear comparison map
2943	  \[
2944	    B \otimes_A \operatorname{eq}(f, g)
2945	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
2946	    \operatorname{eq}\bigl(\mathbf{1}_B \otimes f,\ \mathbf{1}_B \otimes g\bigr)
2947	  \]
2948	  is an isomorphism; that is, base change along a flat algebra commutes with the
2949	  formation of the finite equalizer (equivalently, of the kernel: the case
2950	  \(g = 0\) is \texttt{LinearMap.tensorKerEquiv}, underlain by
2951	  \texttt{Module.Flat.ker\_lTensor\_eq} and \texttt{Module.Flat.eqLocus\_lTensor\_eq}).
2952	  Since \(- \otimes_A B\) also preserves finite products, it follows that
2953	  \(- \otimes_A B\) carries the degree-\(0\)/\(1\) equalizer
2954	  \(\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \rightrightarrows
2955	    \prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})\) of a finite cover to the corresponding
2956	  equalizer of the base-changed cover.
2957	\end{lemma}

3093	  condition (the degree-\(0\)/\(1\) part of {\v C}ech, i.e.\ an equalizer of a finite

3100	  may assume \(S\) and \(S'\) are affine, say \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and

3103	  \(S\); it is therefore the sheaf associated to the \(A\)-module
3104	  \(H^0(S, f_*\mathcal{F}) = H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\). Likewise

3106	  \(H^0(X', \mathcal{F}') = \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). Since pullback along \(g\)
3107	  corresponds to \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules, the base-change map becomes the
3108	  comparison map of \(B\)-modules
3109	  \[
3110	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B),
3111	    \qquad X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)} \operatorname{Spec}(B),
3112	  \]
3113	  and it suffices to prove that this map is an isomorphism.
3114	
3115	  \medskip
3116	  \emph{\(H^0\) as a finite equalizer (the sheaf condition).} Choose a finite affine
3117	  open cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\) (possible since \(X\) is
3118	  quasi-compact). The sheaf condition for \(\mathcal{F}\) on this cover presents the
3119	  global sections as the equalizer of the two restriction maps
3120	  \[
3121	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})
3122	    \;=\;
3123	    \operatorname{eq}\Bigl(
3124	      \textstyle\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F})
3125	      \;\rightrightarrows\;
3126	      \textstyle\prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})
3127	    \Bigr),
3128	    \qquad U_{ij} = U_i \cap U_j,
3129	  \]
3130	  where the two parallel maps are \(s \mapsto (s_i|_{U_{ij}})\) and
3131	  \(s \mapsto (s_j|_{U_{ij}})\). This is precisely Čech degree \(0\) into degree
3132	  \(1\); it is the sheaf axiom and requires \emph{no} separatedness of \(X\) and no
3133	  higher cohomology.

3137	  every intersection \(U_{ij}\) is affine. Base changing the cover yields a finite
3138	  affine cover \(\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \dots \cup (U_t)_B\) of \(X_B\),
3139	  and the same sheaf-condition equalizer presents \(\Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). The
3140	  affine lemma Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward} (in its
3141	  \(i = 0\)/global-sections form) identifies each base-changed term with the original
3142	  term tensored over \(A\) with \(B\):
3143	  \[
3144	    \Gamma\bigl((U_i)_B, \mathcal{F}_B\bigr) \cong \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B,
3145	    \qquad
3146	    \Gamma\bigl((U_{ij})_B, \mathcal{F}_B\bigr) \cong \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B,
3147	  \]
3148	  compatibly with the two restriction maps. Hence the equalizer diagram for \(X_B\) is
3149	  the diagram for \(X\) with \(- \otimes_A B\) applied (using that \(- \otimes_A B\)
3150	  commutes with the finite products \(\prod_i\), \(\prod_{i,j}\)). Because \(A \to B\)
3151	  is flat, base change commutes with the formation of this finite equalizer
3152	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}):
3153	  \[
3154	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B
3155	    = \operatorname{eq}(\cdots) \otimes_A B
3156	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
3157	    \operatorname{eq}(\cdots \otimes_A B)
3158	    = \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B).
3159	  \]
3160	  This is exactly the comparison map of the reduction step, so it is an isomorphism.
3161	
3162	  \medskip

3167	  affine lemma. For \(t > 1\), set \(V = U_t\) (affine) and
3168	  \(W = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_{t-1}\) (an open covered by \(t - 1\) affines). Their
3169	  intersection \(W \cap V = \bigcup_{i < t}(U_i \cap U_t)\) is covered by the
3170	  \(t - 1\) opens \(U_i \cap U_t\), each of which is quasi-compact and separated
3171	  (an open of the affine, hence separated, \(U_i\)); thus \(W \cap V\) is separated
3172	  and quasi-compact, and flat base change holds for it by the separated case. The
3173	  two-member cover \(\{W, V\}\) of \(X\) gives the left-exact Mayer--Vietoris
3174	  sequence — itself the sheaf-condition equalizer for that cover —
3175	  \[

3177	      \longrightarrow \Gamma(W, \mathcal{F}) \oplus \Gamma(V, \mathcal{F})
3178	      \longrightarrow \Gamma(W \cap V, \mathcal{F}),

3184	  \(W \cap V\) (separated), it follows for \(X\). In degree \(0\) this gives
3185	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\), as required.
3186	\end{proof}

1941	\subsubsection*{Subquotient constructors}
1942	
1943	The kernel/cokernel closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}) and the
1944	abstract finiteness transfer (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) are
1945	bundled into two constructors that take a length-\((r+1)\) subquotient datum to the
1946	length-\(r\) kernel and cokernel data consumed by the induction. The two stability
1947	lemmas below first record that the new pairs are stable under the \emph{whole}
1948	endomorphism family (not merely the reduced one), which is what lets the over-\(\kappa[t_0,\dots,t_r]\)
1949	finiteness inputs be stated before the variable is dropped.
1950	
1951	\begin{lemma}
1952	
1953	\leanok
1954	[The kernel pair's lower module is stable under the full family]
1955	  \label{lem:graded_ker_stable_full}
1956	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_stable_full}
1957	  \uses{lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute}
1958	  Let \((N, N')\) be a length-\((r+1)\) subquotient datum
1959	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_r\) and
1960	  \(x = t_r\). Then the kernel pair's lower module \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is stable
1961	  under \emph{every} \(t_i\) (\(0 \le i \le r\)), not only the reduced family: each
1962	  \(t_i\) preserves \(N\) and preserves the preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) because it
1963	  commutes with \(x\) and preserves \(N'\)
1964	  (\cref{lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute}). This full stability is what allows the
1965	  kernel subquotient's finiteness to be measured over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)
1966	  before the transfer drops the last variable.
1967	\end{lemma}
1968	
1969	\begin{lemma}
1970	
1971	\leanok
1972	[The cokernel pair's upper module is stable under the full family]
1973	  \label{lem:graded_coker_stable_full}
1974	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_stable_full}
1975	  \uses{lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute}
1976	  Under the same hypotheses, the cokernel pair's upper module \(N' + x \cdot N\) is
1977	  stable under every \(t_i\) (\(0 \le i \le r\)): each \(t_i\) preserves \(N'\) and
1978	  preserves the image \(x \cdot N\) because it commutes with \(x\) and preserves
1979	  \(N\) (\cref{lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute}), and a map preserves a sum of
1980	  preserved submodules. This full stability supports the over-\(\kappa[t_0, \dots,
1981	  t_r]\) finiteness input of the cokernel constructor.
1982	\end{lemma}
1983	
1984	\begin{definition}
1985	
1986	\leanok
1987	[Kernel constructor of a subquotient datum]
1988	  \label{def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker}
1989	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum.ker}
1990	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1991	    lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_ker_le, lem:graded_ker_annihilate,
1992	    lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute, lem:graded_ker_stable_full,
1993	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer,
1994	    lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_numerator}
1995	  From a length-\((r+1)\) subquotient datum \((N, N')\) with endomorphisms
1996	  \(t_0, \dots, t_r\) and \(x = t_r\) (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}), the
1997	  \emph{kernel constructor} produces the length-\(r\) datum
1998	  \[
1999	    \ker D \;=\; \bigl(\,N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\ N'\,\bigr)
2000	  \]
2001	  on the reduced family \((t_0, \dots, t_{r-1})\). Its
2002	  non-finiteness fields are supplied by the ambient kernel calculus: homogeneity of
2003	  \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous}), the nesting
2004	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_ker_le}), and stability under the
2005	  reduced family (\cref{lem:graded_ker_stable_full}). The finiteness witness
2006	  is obtained by first shrinking the numerator from \(N\) to
2007	  \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_numerator}) to get
2008	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-finiteness of the kernel subquotient, then transferring
2009	  down one variable (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}), valid because
2010	  \(x\) annihilates the kernel subquotient (\cref{lem:graded_ker_annihilate}). This
2011	  realises the kernel half of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker} as a genuine
2012	  length-\(r\) datum.
2013	\end{definition}
2014	
2015	\begin{definition}
2016	
2017	\leanok
2018	[Cokernel constructor of a subquotient datum]
2019	  \label{def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker}
2020	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum.coker}
2021	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
2022	    lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_coker_le, lem:graded_coker_annihilate,
2023	    lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute, lem:graded_coker_stable_full,
2024	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer,
2025	    lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_denominator}
2026	  From the same length-\((r+1)\) datum, the \emph{cokernel constructor} produces the
2027	  length-\(r\) datum
2028	  \[
2029	    \operatorname{coker} D \;=\; \bigl(\,N,\ N' + x \cdot N\,\bigr)
2030	  \]
2031	  on the reduced family. Its non-finiteness fields come from the ambient cokernel
2032	  calculus: homogeneity of \(N' + x \cdot N\) (\cref{lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous}),
2033	  the nesting \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\) (\cref{lem:graded_coker_le}), and stability
2034	  under the reduced family (\cref{lem:graded_coker_stable_full}). The finiteness field
2035	  is obtained by enlarging the denominator from \(N'\) to \(N' + x \cdot N\)
2036	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_denominator}) to get
2037	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-finiteness of the cokernel subquotient, then
2038	  transferring down one variable (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}),
2039	  valid because \(x\) annihilates the cokernel subquotient
2040	  (\cref{lem:graded_coker_annihilate}). This realises the cokernel half of
2041	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker} as a genuine length-\(r\) datum.
2042	\end{definition}

2044	\subsubsection*{The degreewise difference identity}
2045	
2046	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2047	[$D_5$ -- degreewise rank--nullity difference]
2048	  \label{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}
2049	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.degreewise_finrank_diff}
2050	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
2051	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
2052	  Let \(\varphi : V \to W\) be a \(\kappa\)-linear map between finite-dimensional
2053	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. Then
2054	  \[
2055	    \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V
2056	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi).
2057	  \]
2058	  Applied degreewise with \(\varphi = (x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\),
2059	  \(V = \mathcal{M}_n\), \(W = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) -- so that \(\ker\varphi\) is the
2060	  degree-\(n\) piece \(K_n\) of the kernel subquotient and
2061	  \(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x \mathcal{M}_n\) the
2062	  degree-\((n+1)\) piece \(C_{n+1}\) of the cokernel subquotient
2063	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}, via the split \(G_1\)
2064	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq} and the degree shift
2065	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) -- this gives, for every \(n\),
2066	  \[
2067	    \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_n
2068	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa C_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa K_n,
2069	  \]
2070	  which is the difference identity \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) consumed by
2071	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}. No graded structure is used in proving the
2072	  underlying vector-space identity -- it is pure rank--nullity.
2073	\end{lemma}
2074	
2075	\begin{proof}
2076	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
2077	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
2078	    \leanok
2079	  By rank--nullity (\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}),
2080	  \(\dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi) + \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}
2081	  \varphi)\), so \(\dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa V -
2082	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). By additivity on the short exact sequence
2083	  \(0 \to \operatorname{range}\varphi \to W \to W/\operatorname{range}\varphi \to 0\)
2084	  (\cref{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}),
2085	  \(\dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W -
2086	  \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V +
2087	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). Rearranging gives
2088	  \(\dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) -
2089	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). The degreewise application identifies
2090	  \(\ker(x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\) and
2091	  \(\mathcal{M}_{n+1}/\operatorname{range}(x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\)
2092	  with the degree-\(n\) piece of the kernel subquotient and the degree-\((n+1)\)
2093	  piece of the cokernel subquotient (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}),
2094	  the latter because the degree-\((n+1)\) graded piece of the homogeneous submodule
2095	  \(x\mathcal{M}\) is exactly \(x \mathcal{M}_n\)
2096	  (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}).
2097	\end{proof}

2099	\subsubsection*{The base case ($r = 0$)}
2100	
2101	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2102	[Base-case finiteness over the empty polynomial ring]
2103	  \label{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
2104	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
2105	  Let \(Q\) be a \(\kappa\)-vector space that is also a module over
2106	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\sigma, \kappa)\) for an \emph{empty} index set
2107	  \(\sigma\) -- in particular \(\sigma = \{0, \dots, r-1\}\) with \(r = 0\) -- in a way
2108	  compatible with the \(\kappa\)-action (scalar tower). If \(Q\) is module-finite over
2109	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\sigma, \kappa)\), then \(Q\) is finite-dimensional over
2110	  \(\kappa\). Indeed for empty \(\sigma\) the polynomial ring is
2111	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\sigma, \kappa) \cong \kappa\), a finite \(\kappa\)-module,
2112	  so finiteness of \(Q\) over it transfers to finiteness over \(\kappa\) along the scalar
2113	  tower. This is the length-zero input to the ambient subquotient induction
2114	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}): when \(r = 0\) the subquotient \(N/N'\) is a
2115	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space, hence \(\mathrm{hilb}\) is eventually zero.
2116	\end{lemma}
2117	
2118	\begin{lemma}
2119	
2120	\leanok
2121	[independence of images modulo the kernel]
2122	  \label{lem:graded_iSupIndep_map_of_mem_ker_sup}
2123	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.iSupIndep_map_of_mem_ker_sup}
2124	  Let \(\pi : A \to Q\) be a \(\kappa\)-linear map and \((g_i)_{i \in \iota}\) a family of
2125	  subspaces of \(A\) that is \emph{independent modulo \(\ker\pi\)}: for each \(i\), every
2126	  \(a \in g_i\) that also lies in \(\ker\pi + \sum_{j \ne i} g_j\) already lies in
2127	  \(\ker\pi\). Then the image family \((\pi(g_i))_{i \in \iota}\) is independent
2128	  (\(\mathrm{iSupIndep}\)) in \(Q\). Ring-agnostic lattice core of the base-case
2129	  independence step below; applied to the quotient map \(N \twoheadrightarrow N/N'\).
2130	\end{lemma}
2131	\begin{proof}
2132	
2133	\leanok
2134	  By the disjointness criterion for independence it suffices to show, for each \(i\),
2135	  that \(\pi(g_i) \cap \sum_{j \ne i} \pi(g_j) = 0\). Take \(q\) in that intersection;
2136	  write \(q = \pi(a)\) with \(a \in g_i\), and (pushing the sum through \(\pi\), since
2137	  \(\pi(\sum_{j\ne i} g_j) = \sum_{j\ne i}\pi(g_j)\)) \(q = \pi(b)\) with
2138	  \(b \in \sum_{j \ne i} g_j\). Then \(a - b \in \ker\pi\), so
2139	  \(a = (a-b) + b \in \ker\pi + \sum_{j \ne i} g_j\). By the hypothesis applied at \(i\),
2140	  \(a \in \ker\pi\), whence \(q = \pi(a) = 0\).
2141	\end{proof}
2142	
2143	\begin{lemma}
2144	
2145	\leanok
2146	[Base case: the length-zero subquotient Hilbert function vanishes eventually]
2147	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero}
2148	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_base_eventuallyZero}
2149	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb,
2150	    lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite,
2151	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep, lem:graded_iSupIndep_map_of_mem_ker_sup,
2152	    lem:finite_ne_bot_of_iSupIndep_mathlib, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
2153	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
2154	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
2155	  % 00K1), proof, base case (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex,
2156	  % L13908-L13910).
2157	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2158	  % "We prove this by induction on the minimal number of
2159	  %  generators of $S_1$. If this number is $0$, then
2160	  %  $M_n = 0$ for all $n \gg 0$ and the result holds."
2161	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (base case of the induction).}
2162	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum of length \(0\)
2163	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}): a homogeneous pair \(N' \le N\) of the fixed
2164	  ambient graded module \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) with \emph{no} endomorphisms,
2165	  whose finiteness witness says the subquotient \(Q_0 := N/N'\) is module-finite over
2166	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\varnothing, \kappa)\). Then the ambient Hilbert
2167	  function vanishes eventually: there is \(K \in \mathbb{N}\) with
2168	  \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = 0\) for all \(n > K\). Consequently
2169	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\) (\cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}),
2170	  which is the base case of the ambient subquotient induction
2171	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}).
2172	\end{lemma}
2173	
2174	\begin{proof}
2175	  \uses{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite,
2176	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
2177	    lem:finite_ne_bot_of_iSupIndep_mathlib, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
2178	    \leanok
2179	  Since the index set of variables is empty,
2180	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\varnothing, \kappa) \cong \kappa\), so the finiteness
2181	  witness makes the subquotient \(Q_0 = N/N'\) a \emph{finite-dimensional}
2182	  \(\kappa\)-vector space
2183	  (\cref{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}). For each
2184	  degree \(n\), composing the inclusion of the homogeneous piece
2185	  \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n \hookrightarrow N\) with the quotient projection
2186	  \(N \twoheadrightarrow Q_0\) gives a \(\kappa\)-linear map
2187	  \(\psi_n : N \cap \mathcal{M}_n \to Q_0\), \(m \mapsto [m]\), whose image is the
2188	  class of the degree-\(n\) homogeneous piece inside the subquotient.
2189	
2190	  \emph{Step 1: independence of the images.} The family
2191	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n)\bigr)_{n}\) is \(\mathrm{iSupIndep}\) in
2192	  \(Q_0\). The reason is the ambient direct-sum grading of \(M\): the homogeneous
2193	  pieces \(\mathcal{M}_n\) form an independent family
2194	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep}). An element of
2195	  \(\bigsqcup_{j \ne n} \operatorname{range}(\psi_j)\) is a finite sum of classes of
2196	  homogeneous elements of degrees \(j \ne n\); since \(N'\) is homogeneous, the
2197	  degree-\(n\) homogeneous component of any representative of such a class lies in
2198	  \(N'\), so the class has zero degree-\(n\) component in \(Q_0\). A nonzero class in
2199	  \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n)\), by contrast, is represented by a homogeneous
2200	  element of degree \(n\) not in \(N'\), so has nonzero degree-\(n\) component.
2201	  Comparing degree-\(n\) components forces the intersection
2202	  \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n) \cap \bigsqcup_{j \ne n} \operatorname{range}(\psi_j)
2203	  = 0\); independence follows.
2204	  % NOTE: ROUTE (b). Prove the iSupIndep by direct membership analysis in
2205	  %   ⨆_{j ≠ n} range(ψ j), reading off the degree-n homogeneous component
2206	  %   directly inside Q₀'s fixed κ-module structure.
2207	  %   No outgoing linear map out of Q₀ is constructed, so no scalar ring other than κ
2208	  %   is ever introduced.
2209	  % NOTE: ROUTE (a). Building a κ-linear detector
2210	  %   Φ : Q₀ → M/N', [m] ↦ [π_n m] (degree-n projection) via descent through the
2211	  %   subquotient fails: the descended map carries the scalar action of Q₀'s base
2212	  %   ring, but the target M/N' carries only a κ-module structure -- a scalar-ring
2213	  %   incompatibility. Route (b) avoids this by working entirely within Q₀.
2214	
2215	  \emph{Step 2: finiteness of the support.} Because \(Q_0\) is finite-dimensional and
2216	  the ranges \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n)\) are independent, only finitely many of
2217	  them are nonzero: the set \(\{\,n : \operatorname{range}(\psi_n) \ne 0\,\}\) is
2218	  finite (\cref{lem:finite_ne_bot_of_iSupIndep_mathlib}). In particular it is bounded
2219	  above, by some \(K \in \mathbb{N}\).
2220	
2221	  \emph{Step 3: eventual vanishing.} For \(n > K\) we have
2222	  \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n) = 0\), i.e.\ every homogeneous element of
2223	  \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\) has class \(0\) in \(Q_0 = N/N'\); equivalently
2224	  \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n \le N'\), hence \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n = N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n\)
2225	  and
2226	  \[
2227	    \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; \dim_\kappa(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
2228	      - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n) \;=\; 0 .
2229	  \]
2230	  Thus \(\mathrm{hilb}\) is eventually zero, and
2231	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero} gives \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\).
2232	\end{proof}

2234	\subsubsection*{The ambient subquotient induction}
2235	
2236	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2237	[Rationality of the ambient subquotient Hilbert function]
2238	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}
2239	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_hilbertSeries_rational}
2240	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
2241	    def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker, def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker,
2242	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
2243	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero,
2244	    lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq, lem:ratHilb_bump,
2245	    lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero,
2246	    lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
2247	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
2248	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
2249	  % 00K1), proof (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13908-L13914).
2250	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (induction on the number of
2251	  degree-one generators).}
2252	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum of length \(r\)
2253	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with ambient Hilbert function
2254	  \(\mathrm{hilb}\). Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, r)\)
2255	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}): the difference \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
2256	  - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), the coefficient
2257	  sequence of a rational series \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-r}\) with \(p \in
2258	  \mathbb{Q}[X]\). The induction is on the length \(r\), and every object that
2259	  appears is an ambient pair of homogeneous submodules of the fixed graded module
2260	  \(M\).
2261	\end{lemma}
2262	
2263	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, induction set-up (read
2264	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13908-L13914).
2265	% "We prove this by induction on the minimal number of
2266	%  generators of $S_1$. If this number is $0$, then
2267	%  $M_n = 0$ for all $n \gg 0$ and the result holds.
2268	%  To prove the induction step, let $x\in S_1$
2269	%  be one of a minimal set of generators, such that
2270	%  the induction hypothesis applies to the
2271	%  graded ring $S/(x)$."
2272	\begin{proof}
2273	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
2274	    def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker, def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker,
2275	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
2276	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero,
2277	    lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq, lem:ratHilb_bump,
2278	    lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero,
2279	    lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
2280	    \leanok
2281	  We induct on the length \(r\).
2282	
2283	  \emph{Base case \(r = 0\).} A length-zero datum has a finiteness witness making the
2284	  subquotient \(N/N'\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space
2285	  (\cref{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}); its degreewise
2286	  images form an independent family inside that finite-dimensional space, so only
2287	  finitely many are nonzero and \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = 0\) for \(n \gg 0\)
2288	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero}). By
2289	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero} an eventually-zero function is rational of order
2290	  \(0\), giving \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\).
2291	
2292	  \emph{Inductive step \(r \ge 1\).} Set \(x = t_{r-1}\). By the kernel/cokernel
2293	  closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}) the kernel subquotient
2294	  \(K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and the cokernel subquotient
2295	  \(C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) are subquotient data of length \(r-1\), assembled by
2296	  the kernel and cokernel constructors (\cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker},
2297	  \cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker}) whose finiteness witnesses come from the
2298	  abstract finiteness transfer (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}). The
2299	  induction hypothesis applied
2300	  to \(K\) and \(C\) gives \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, r-1)\) and
2301	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, r-1)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}). By the degreewise
2302	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}),
2303	  \[
2304	    \mathrm{hilb}(n+1) - \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)
2305	    \qquad \text{for all } n .
2306	  \]
2307	  Feeding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, r-1)\), \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, r-1)\) and this
2308	  difference identity into the inductive-step engine
2309	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}) yields \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, r)\). (The
2310	  common order \(r-1\) for \(h_C\) and \(h_K\) is arranged, if necessary, by raising
2311	  the lower one with \cref{lem:ratHilb_bump}.) This completes the induction.
2312	\end{proof}
2313	
2314	\section{Support and freeness predicates}
2315	\label{sec:quot_predicates}
2316	
2317	The Quot functor (\cref{def:quot_functor}) and the Grassmannian
2318	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) classify quotient sheaves subject to two
2319	geometric conditions: a coherent quotient must have \emph{schematic support
2320	proper over the base}, and the Grassmannian's quotients must be \emph{locally
2321	free of a fixed rank}. This section introduces both predicates.
2322	
2323	\begin{definition}
2324	
2325	\leanok
2326	[Annihilator ideal sheaf of a sheaf of modules]
2327	  \label{def:modules_annihilator}
2328	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator}
2329	  \uses{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
2330	  % NOTE: The definition itself is formalized and axiom-clean; the inclusion direction
2331	  % \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U) \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\)
2332	  % (\cref{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}) is closed. The reverse inclusion, which identifies
2333	  % the annihilator sheaf with the module-annihilator on affine opens, remains bridge-gated on
2334	  % \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}.
2335	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
2336	  % Polynomials" (read from
2337	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
2338	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2339	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
2340	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
2341	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
2342	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
2343	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
2344	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of
2345	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules that is \emph{quasi-coherent and of finite type}
2346	  (over a locally noetherian base, so that on each affine open \(U\) the section
2347	  module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is a finitely generated \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module).
2348	  The \emph{annihilator ideal sheaf} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the ideal sheaf
2349	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) defined on affine opens: for each affine open
2350	  \(U \subseteq X\),
2351	  \[
2352	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
2353	    \;=\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr)
2354	    \;\subseteq\; \mathcal{O}_X(U),
2355	  \]
2356	  the annihilator of the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\). These
2357	  local data patch to a coherent ideal sheaf on \(X\) whose vanishing locus is
2358	  the set-theoretic support of \(\mathcal{F}\).
2359	
2360	  \emph{Well-definedness on basic opens.} The coherence condition for an ideal
2361	  sheaf requires that, for an affine open \(U\) and \(f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
2362	  the section \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)\) restricts correctly to the basic
2363	  open \(D(f) \subseteq U\), i.e. that
2364	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(D(f))}(\mathcal{F}(D(f)))\) is the extension of
2365	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\) along the localization map
2366	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \to \mathcal{O}_X(D(f))\). This is exactly where the two
2367	  hypotheses enter. The bridge
2368	  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} supplies, for a quasi-coherent
2369	  finite-type \(\mathcal{F}\), that \(\mathcal{O}_X(D(f)) = (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\)
2370	  and that the restriction \(\mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{F}(D(f))\) exhibits
2371	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(f))\) as the localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
2372	  \mathcal{F}(U)\); the algebra engine
2373	  \cref{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map} then yields, using that
2374	  \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is finitely generated, the required identity
2375	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{(\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f}\bigl((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
2376	  \mathcal{F}(U)\bigr) = \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U)) \cdot
2377	  (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\). Thus the local annihilators are compatible with basic-open
2378	  restriction, which is the patching datum that makes \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\)
2379	  a well-defined ideal sheaf. The finite-type hypothesis is genuinely required:
2380	  without finite generation of \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) the annihilator need not commute
2381	  with localization, and the local data fail to patch.
2382	
2383	  \emph{Note.} Mathlib does not carry an annihilator for sheaves of modules on a scheme.
2384	\end{definition}
2385	
2386	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2387	[Annihilator ideal bounded by the module annihilator on affine opens]
2388	  \label{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}
2389	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator_ideal_le}
2390	  \uses{def:modules_annihilator}

2392	  \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open. Then the section over \(U\) of the annihilator
2393	  ideal sheaf \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}) is
2394	  contained in the module-annihilator of the sections of \(\mathcal{F}\) over
2395	  \(U\):
2396	  \[
2397	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
2398	    \;\subseteq\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr).
2399	  \]
2400	  This is the inclusion direction available directly from the definition of
2401	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\); the reverse inclusion is the basic-open coherence
2402	  statement gated on \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}. Proved directly
2403	  in Lean. It supports the well-definedness of the schematic support
2404	  (\cref{def:schematic_support}).
2405	\end{lemma}
2406	
2407	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2408	[Annihilator of a finitely generated module commutes with
2409	  localization]
2410	  \label{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map}
2411	  \lean{Module.annihilator_isLocalizedModule_eq_map}
2412	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(S \subseteq R\) be a multiplicative
2413	  submonoid, and let \(R_S = S^{-1}R\) be the localization of \(R\) at \(S\). Let
2414	  \(M\) be a \emph{finitely generated} \(R\)-module and let \(f : M \to M_S\) be
2415	  an \(R\)-linear map exhibiting \(M_S\) as the localized module \(S^{-1}M\)
2416	  (i.e.\ \(f\) is a localization of \(M\) at \(S\)). Then the annihilator of
2417	  \(M_S\) over \(R_S\) is the extension of the annihilator of \(M\) along the
2418	  structure map \(R \to R_S\):
2419	  \[
2420	    \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)
2421	    \;=\; (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S
2422	    \;=\; \mathrm{map}\bigl(R \to R_S\bigr)\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}_R M\bigr).
2423	  \]
2424	  Equivalently, for finitely generated \(M\) the annihilator commutes with
2425	  localization: \(\mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M) = S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M)\).
2426	
2427	  \emph{The finite-generation hypothesis is essential.} For a module that is not
2428	  finitely generated the inclusion \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M) \subseteq
2429	  \mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M)\) can be strict, so the identity fails. This is the
2430	  standard commutative-algebra fact that the annihilator of a finite module
2431	  localizes; it is recorded here as the load-bearing algebra engine for the
2432	  basic-open coherence of \cref{def:modules_annihilator}.
2433	\end{lemma}
2434	
2435	\begin{proof}
2436	  \(\supseteq\): if \(r \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\), then \(r\) kills every element of
2437	  \(M\), hence its image \(r/1 \in R_S\) kills every element of \(M_S\) (each such
2438	  element is \(m/s = (1/s)\cdot f(m)\) for some \(m \in M\), \(s \in S\), and
2439	  \((r/1)\cdot(m/s) = (1/s)\cdot f(r\,m) = 0\)). Thus
2440	  \((\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\).
2441	
2442	  \(\subseteq\): write \(M = \langle m_1, \dots, m_n\rangle\) using the
2443	  finite-generation hypothesis, and let \(y \in \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\). Write
2444	  \(y = a/s\) with \(a \in R\), \(s \in S\). For each generator \(m_i\) the element
2445	  \(y\cdot f(m_i) = (a/s)\cdot f(m_i) = 0\) in \(M_S\); by the definition of the
2446	  localized module this means there is \(u_i \in S\) with \(u_i\,a\,m_i = 0\) in
2447	  \(M\). Put \(u = \prod_{i=1}^n u_i \in S\). Then \(u\,a\) kills every generator
2448	  \(m_i\), hence kills all of \(M\), so \(u\,a \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\). Finally
2449	  \(y = a/s = (u\,a)/(u\,s)\) lies in \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}_R M =
2450	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\). Therefore \(\mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S) \subseteq
2451	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\), and combined with the reverse inclusion the two
2452	  ideals coincide. The use of a single common multiplier \(u = \prod_i u_i\) is
2453	  exactly the step that requires finitely many generators.
2454	\end{proof}
2455

2490	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2491	  % "Let $R$ be a ring. Let $M$ be an $R$-module. Let $\widetilde M$

2498	  \(\widetilde M\) on \(\Spec(R)\), item (4).}
2499	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a \emph{quasi-coherent} sheaf
2500	  of modules on \(X\). Let \(U \subseteq X\) be an
2501	  affine open and \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then:
2502	  \begin{enumerate}

2504	      of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\)

2508	      \(\mathcal{M}(D(f))\) as the localized module
2509	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\mathcal{M}(U)\); that is, it is a localization
2510	      of the \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(\mathcal{M}(U)\) at the submonoid

2515	\begin{proof}
2516	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
2517	  Part (1) is \cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}, the Mathlib statement
2518	  that on an affine open \(U\) the sections over the basic open \(D(f)\) form the
2519	  localization of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\).
2520	
2521	  For part (2), identify the affine open \(U\) with \(\Spec \Gamma(X, U)\). Over
2522	  this affine, a quasi-coherent sheaf is \(\widetilde{N}\) for the
2523	  \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\), the sheaf associated to
2524	  \(N\) (no finiteness of \(N\) is required for this step). The Spec-local
2525	  computation of the sections of \(\widetilde{N}\) on a
2526	  basic open gives \(\Gamma\bigl(D(f), \widetilde{N}\bigr) = N_f\) as an
2527	  \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)-module, and the restriction map
2528	  \(\Gamma(\Spec \Gamma(X,U), \widetilde{N}) = N \to N_f\) is precisely the
2529	  localization homomorphism \(N \to (\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1} N\). Transporting
2530	  this identification across the affine isomorphism
2531	  \(U \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, U)\) -- under which \(\mathcal{M}|_U\) corresponds to
2532	  \(\widetilde{N}\) by quasi-coherence and the basic open \(D(f) \subseteq U\)

2534	  that the restriction \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}(D(f))\) is the
2535	  localization of \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\), i.e.\ it is a
2536	  localized module at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\). The substantive content is this
2537	  transport of the Spec-local localization fact for the sheaf \(\widetilde{N}\)
2538	  to a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{M}\) via the affine identification.

2908	  \(S\) are canonically isomorphic by a linear equivalence \(M' \cong M''\) compatible with \(g\)
2909	  and \(g'\).
2910	\end{lemma}

2915	  \mathlibok

2918	  the localization map.

2923	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2924	[A map intertwining two localizations is bijective]
2925	  \label{lem:bijective_comp_of_localizations}
2926	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.bijective_comp_of_localizations}
2927	  \uses{lem:isLocalizedModule_linearEquiv_mathlib, lem:isLocalizedModule_linearMap_ext_mathlib}
2928	  \textit{Linear-algebra glue for the G1-assemble step.}
2929	  Let \(S \subseteq R\) be a submonoid and let \(g : M \to M'\), \(g' : M \to M''\) both exhibit a
2930	  localization of \(M\) at \(S\). If a linear map \(h : M' \to M''\) satisfies \(h \circ g = g'\),
2931	  then \(h\) is bijective.
2932	\end{lemma}
2933	\begin{proof}
2934	  \uses{lem:isLocalizedModule_linearEquiv_mathlib, lem:isLocalizedModule_linearMap_ext_mathlib}

2936	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule.linearEquiv}\,S\,g\,g'\)
2937	  (\cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_linearEquiv_mathlib}): both compose with \(g\) to give \(g'\), so they
2938	  coincide by uniqueness of maps out of a localization
2939	  (\cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_linearMap_ext_mathlib}). A linear equivalence is bijective, hence so
2940	  is \(h\).
2941	\end{proof}

2943	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2944	[Basis-wise isomorphism of sheaves on $\Spec R$]
2945	  \label{lem:isIso_sheaf_of_isIso_app_basicOpen}
2946	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_sheaf_of_isIso_app_basicOpen}
2947	  \uses{lem:isBasis_basic_opens_mathlib, lem:stalkFunctor_map_injective_of_isBasis_mathlib,
2948	    lem:isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso_mathlib}
2949	  \textit{Upgrades a per-basic-open isomorphism to an isomorphism of sheaves.}
2950	  Let \(\alpha : F \to G\) be a morphism of sheaves of \(R\)-modules on \(\Spec R\) whose component
2951	  on every basic open \(D(f)\) is an isomorphism. Then \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism.
2952	\end{lemma}
2953	\begin{proof}
2954	  \uses{lem:isBasis_basic_opens_mathlib, lem:stalkFunctor_map_injective_of_isBasis_mathlib,
2955	    lem:isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso_mathlib}
2956	  The basic opens form a basis of \(\Spec R\) (\cref{lem:isBasis_basic_opens_mathlib}). Bijectivity
2957	  of every component \(\alpha_{D(f)}\) gives, in particular, injectivity on each basis open, hence
2958	  injectivity on every stalk (\cref{lem:stalkFunctor_map_injective_of_isBasis_mathlib});
2959	  surjectivity on stalks follows by lifting germs through the bijective basic-open components. A
2960	  morphism that is an isomorphism on every stalk is an isomorphism
2961	  (\cref{lem:isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso_mathlib}).
2962	\end{proof}

2964	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2965	[$\mathrm{IsIso}\,M.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}$ from per-basic-open section localization
2966	(G1-assemble)]
2967	  \label{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}
2968	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}
2969	  \uses{lem:isIso_sheaf_of_isIso_app_basicOpen, lem:bijective_comp_of_localizations,
2970	    lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict}
2971	  \textit{The G1-assemble reduction: section-localization on every basic open forces the
2972	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\)-counit to be an isomorphism.}
2973	  Let \(M\) be a sheaf of modules on \(\Spec R\), and suppose that for every \(f \in R\) the section
2974	  restriction \(\Gamma(M, \top) \to \Gamma(M, D(f))\) exhibits the target as
2975	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) over \(R\) -- exactly the conclusion of G1-core
2976	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization}). Then the counit \(M.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) is an
2977	  isomorphism.
2978	\end{lemma}
2979	\begin{proof}
2980	  \uses{lem:isIso_sheaf_of_isIso_app_basicOpen, lem:bijective_comp_of_localizations,
2981	    lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict}
2982	  Write \(N = \Gamma(M, \top)\). On each basic open \(D(f)\), the component of
2983	  \(\mathrm{modulesSpecToSheaf}(M.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ})\) is a map between two localizations of \(N\)
2984	  at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\): the source \(\Gamma(\widetilde N, D(f))\) localizes \(N\) via the affine
2985	  tilde map (\cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict}) and the target \(\Gamma(M, D(f))\)
2986	  localizes \(N\) by hypothesis, the two being intertwined by the compatibility
2987	  \(\mathrm{toOpen\_fromTildeΓ\_app}\). Hence the component is bijective
2988	  (\cref{lem:bijective_comp_of_localizations}), so an isomorphism. By
2989	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheaf_of_isIso_app_basicOpen} the sheaf morphism
2990	  \(\mathrm{modulesSpecToSheaf}(M.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ})\) is then an isomorphism, and since
2991	  \(\mathrm{modulesSpecToSheaf}\) is fully faithful it reflects isomorphisms, giving
2992	  \(\mathrm{IsIso}\,M.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\).

3022	    def:schematic_support_immersion}

3024	  % Polynomials" (read from
3025	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
3026	  % SOURCE QUOTE:

3031	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}

3033	  \emph{schematic support} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the closed subscheme of \(X\)

3035	  \[

3038	  \]

3044	\begin{definition}\leanok
3045	[Schematic-support immersion]
3046	  \label{def:schematic_support_immersion}
3047	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.schematicSupportι}
3048	  \uses{def:schematic_support, def:modules_annihilator}
3049	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\). The
3050	  \emph{schematic-support immersion} is the canonical closed immersion
3051	  \[
3052	    \mathrm{schematicSupport}(\mathcal{F}) \;\hookrightarrow\; X
3053	  \]
3054	  of the schematic support (\cref{def:schematic_support}) into the ambient
3055	  scheme, namely the subscheme immersion of the annihilator ideal sheaf
3056	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}). It is a
3057	  quasi-compact preimmersion onto the support, and it is the morphism whose
3058	  composite with a base morphism \(f : X \to S\) defines proper support
3059	  (\cref{def:has_proper_support}). Proved directly in Lean.
3060	\end{definition}

3062	\begin{lemma}[Properness as a morphism property]
3063	  \label{lem:isProper_mathlib}
3064	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsProper}
3065	  \mathlibok
3066	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
3067	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Proper}).}
3068	  A morphism \(f : X \to S\) of schemes is \emph{proper} iff it is separated,
3069	  universally closed, and locally of finite type. Properness is stable under base
3070	  change: if \(f : X \to S\) is proper and \(g : T \to S\) is any morphism then
3071	  the base change \(X \times_S T \to T\) is proper. Consequently the base-change
3072	  clause that Nitsure invokes (``properness \ldots\ preserved by base-change'')
3073	  applies directly to the proper-support condition below.
3074	\end{lemma}

3077	
3078	\leanok
3079	[Proper support over the base]
3080	  \label{def:has_proper_support}
3081	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.HasProperSupport}
3082	  \uses{def:schematic_support, def:schematic_support_immersion, lem:isProper_mathlib}
3083	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors $\Hilb_{X/S}$ and
3084	  % $\Quot_{E/X/S}$" (read from
3085	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L407-L409
3086	  % + L418-L419).
3087	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
3088	  % "(i) a coherent sheaf $\F$ on $X_T = X\times_ST$
3089	  %  such that the schematic support of $\F$ is proper over $T$
3090	  %  and $\F$ is flat over $T$, together with
3091	  %  [...]
3092	  %  Then as properness and flatness are preserved
3093	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,"
3094	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
3095	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\) and let
3096	  \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism. We say \(\mathcal{F}\) \emph{has proper support
3097	  over \(S\) (along \(f\))} when the composite morphism
3098	  \[
3099	    \mathrm{schematicSupport}(\mathcal{F}) \hookrightarrow X \xrightarrow{f} S
3100	  \]
3101	  is proper (\cref{lem:isProper_mathlib}). Since properness is stable under base
3102	  change (\cref{lem:isProper_mathlib}), this condition is preserved by pull-back
3103	  along any \(S'\to S\), which is exactly what the well-definedness of the Quot

3121	  %  the set of all equivalence classes $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients

3133	\section{The Quot functor}
3134	\label{sec:quot_functor}
3135	
3136	\begin{definition}\leanok
3137	[Quot functor]
3138	  \label{def:quot_functor}
3139	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotFunctor}
3140	  \uses{def:hilbert_polynomial, def:has_proper_support}
3141	
3142	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors \(\Hilb_{X/S}\) and
3143	  % \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\)" (read from
3144	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L394-L433
3145	  % + L481-L498).
3146	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
3147	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(X\to S\) be a finite type
3148	  %  scheme over it. Let \(E\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X\).
3149	  %  Let \(Sch_S\) denote the category of all locally noetherian schemes
3150	  %  over \(S\). For any \(T\to S\) in \(Sch_S\), a \textbf{family of quotients of \(E\)
3151	  %  parametrised by \(T\)} will mean
3152	  %  a pair \((\F,q)\) consisting of
3153	  %
3154	  %  (i) a coherent sheaf \(\F\) on \(X_T = X\times_ST\)
3155	  %  such that the schematic support of \(\F\) is proper over \(T\)
3156	  %  and \(\F\) is flat over \(T\), together with
3157	  %
3158	  %  (ii) a surjective \({\mathcal O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism of sheaves
3159	  %  \(q:E_T  \to \F\) where \(E_T\) is the pull-back of \(E\) under
3160	  %  the projection \(X_T \to X\).
3161	  %
3162	  %  Two such families \((\F,q)\) and \((\F,q)\) parametrised by \(T\) will
3163	  %  be regarded as equivalent if \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\)),
3164	  %  and \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) will denote
3165	  %  an equivalence class. Then as properness and flatness are preserved
3166	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,
3167	  %  the pull-back of \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) under an \(S\)-morphism \(T'\to T\) is
3168	  %  well-defined, which gives
3169	  %  a set-valued contravariant functor
3170	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S} : Sch_S \to Sets\) under which
3171	  %  \(\)T\mapsto  \{\mbox{ All } \langle \F, q \rangle \mbox{ parametrised by }T\}\(\)
3172	  %  [...]
3173	  %  This shows that the functor \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\) naturally decomposes
3174	  %  as a co-product
3175	  %  \(\)\Quot_{E/X/S} = \coprod_{{\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]}\, \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\(\)
3176	  %  where for any polynomial \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\), the functor
3177	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) associates
3178	  %  to any \(T\) the set of all equivalence classes of
3179	  %  families \(\langle \F,q \rangle\) such that at each \(t\in T\) the
3180	  %  Hilbert polynomial of the restriction \(\F_t\), calculated
3181	  %  using the pull-back of \(L\), is \({\Phi}\)."
3182	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
3183	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
3184	  \(L\) a line bundle on \(X\), \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\), and
3185	  \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). The \emph{Quot functor with Hilbert
3186	  polynomial \(\Phi\)}
3187	  \[
3188	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set}
3189	  \]
3190	  sends an \(S\)-scheme \(T \to S\) to the set of equivalence classes
3191	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) of pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) where
3192	  \begin{enumerate}
3193	    \item \(\mathcal{F}\) is a coherent sheaf on \(X_T = X \times_S T\) whose
3194	      schematic support is proper over \(T\) (\cref{def:has_proper_support}) and
3195	      which is flat over \(T\);
3196	    \item \(q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) is a surjective
3197	      \(\mathcal{O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism, where \(E_T\) is the pull-back
3198	      of \(E\) along \(X_T \to X\);
3199	    \item for every \(t \in T\) the Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}\)
3200	      with respect to \(L|_{X_t}\) (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}) equals
3201	      \(\Phi\).
3202	  \end{enumerate}
3203	  Two pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) and \((\mathcal{F}', q')\) are equivalent iff
3204	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). On morphisms \(f : T' \to T\) over \(S\), the functor
3205	  acts by pulling back the quotient along \(X_{T'} \to X_T\) (well-defined
3206	  since tensor product is right-exact and preserves both flatness and
3207	  properness). The Hilbert scheme is the special case
3208	  \(\Hilb^{\Phi,L}_{X/S} := \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\).
3209	\end{definition}

3211	\section{The Grassmannian scheme}
3212	\label{sec:grassmannian_scheme}
3213	
3214	The Quot construction proceeds by embedding \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) as a
3215	locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian over \(S\). Since Mathlib carries
3216	no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} (only a finite-rank-quotient variety in
3217	linear algebra), we package the construction here.
3218	
3219	\begin{definition}\leanok
3220	[Grassmannian scheme]
3221	  \label{def:grassmannian_scheme}
3222	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian}
3223	  \uses{def:quot_functor, def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
3224	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Elementary Examples, Exercises~(2)" and
3225	  % "Construction of Grassmannian" (read from
3226	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L599
3227	  % + L773-L850).
3228	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
3229	  % "For any integers \(r\ge d\ge 1\), an explicit construction
3230	  %  the Grassmannian scheme
3231	  %  \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\), together with the tautological
3232	  %  quotient \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) where \
3233	  %  \(\U\) is a rank \(d\) locally free sheaf on \(\grass(r,d)\),
3234	  %  has been given at the end of this section. [...]
3235	  %  Show that \(\grass(r,d)\) together with the quotient
3236	  %  \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) represents the
3237	  %  contravariant functor
3238	  %  \(\)\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}\(\)
3239	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any \(T\) the
3240	  %  set of all equivalence classes
3241	  %  \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) of quotients \(q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F\)
3242	  %  where \(\F\) is a locally free sheaf on \(T\) of rank \(d\).
3243	  %  [...]
3244	  %  Using the above show that, more generally, if \(S\) is a scheme and
3245	  %  \(E\) is a locally free \({\mathcal O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\),
3246	  %  the functor \(\Grass(E,d)=\Quot_{E/S/S}^{d,{\mathcal O}_S}\)
3247	  %  on all \(S\)-schemes which
3248	  %  by definition associates to any \(T\)
3249	  %  the set of all equivalence classes \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) of quotients
3250	  %  \(q: E_T \to \F\) where \(\F\) is a locally free sheaf on \(T\) of rank \(d\),
3251	  %  is representable. The representing scheme
3252	  %  is denoted by \(\grass(E,d)\) and is called the rank \(d\)
3253	  %  relative Grassmannian of \(E\) over \(S\)."
3254	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2) and "Construction of
3255	  Grassmannian" (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
3256	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(V\) be a locally free
3257	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\). For an integer \(1 \le d \le r\), the
3258	  \emph{Grassmannian of rank-\(d\) quotients of \(V\)} is the functor
3259	  \[
3260	    \mathrm{Grass}(V, d) : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set},
3261	    \qquad
3262	    T \longmapsto \{ \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle :
3263	    q : V_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F},\; \mathcal{F}
3264	    \text{ locally free of rank } d \}
3265	  \]
3266	  with equivalence \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\); the rank-\(d\) local freeness of
3267	  \(\mathcal{F}\) is as in \cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}. Concretely,
3268	  \(\mathrm{Grass}(V, d) = \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_S}_{V/S/S}\): the Quot functor
3269	  (\cref{def:quot_functor}) in the special case \(X = S\), \(E = V\), constant
3270	  Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi = d\).
3271	\end{definition}
3272	
3273	\begin{lemma}[Representability predicate]
3274	  \label{lem:functor_is_representable_mathlib}
3275	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.IsRepresentable}

3313	  %  quotient \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) where \
3314	  %  \(\U\) is a rank \(d\) locally free sheaf on \(\grass(r,d)\),

3316	  %  the schemes
3317	  %  \(U^I\), as \(I\) varies over all the

3323	  %  \(\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z\) is smooth of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\).
3324	  %  [...]
3325	  %  We now show that \(\pi : \grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z\) is proper. It is enough to
3326	  %  verify the valuative criterion of properness for discrete valuation rings.
3327	  %  [...]
3328	  %  As \(\U\) is given by the transition functions \(g_{I,J}\) described above,
3329	  %  the determinant line bundle
3330	  %  \(\det(\U)\) is given by the transition functions
3331	  %  \(\det(g_{I,J}) = 1/P^I_J \in GL_1(U^I_J)\).
3332	  %  [...]
3333	  %  We leave it to the reader to verify that the sections \(\sigma_I\) form
3334	  %  a linear system which is base point free and separates points
3335	  %  relative to \(\spec \Z\), and so gives an embedding of
3336	  %  \(\grass(r,d)\) into \(\PP^m_{\Z}\) where \(m = {r\choose d}-1\).
3337	  %  This is a closed embedding by the properness of
3338	  %  \(\pi:\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z\). In particular, \(\det(\U)\)

3340	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Construction of Grassmannian" (FGA
3341	  Explained Ch.~5).}
3342	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(V\) a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module
3343	  of rank \(r\), and \(1 \le d \le r\). The functor \(\mathrm{Grass}(V,d)\) of
3344	  \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} is representable by a smooth projective
3345	  \(S\)-scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \to S\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\),
3346	  equipped with a tautological quotient
3347	  \(\pi^* V \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). The determinant
3348	  line bundle \(\det(\mathcal{U})\) is relatively very ample, and the
3349	  associated linear system gives a closed embedding
3350	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \hookrightarrow
3351	  \mathbb{P}_S\!\left(\bigwedge^d V\right)\) -- the \emph{Pl\"ucker embedding}.
3352	
3353	\end{theorem}
3354	
3355	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the "Construction of Grassmannian" sub-section of
3356	% [Nitsure], \S 1 (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
3357	% L773-L930), which gives the construction in five named steps
3358	% ("Construction by gluing together affine patches", "Separatedness",
3359	% "Properness", "Universal quotient", "Projective embedding"). The total
3360	% verbatim text is ~150 lines; it is reproduced piecewise in the
3361	% SOURCE QUOTE comment of the theorem statement above.
3362	\begin{proof}
3363	  
3364	  Over \(S = \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) and \(V = \mathcal{O}_S^{\oplus r}\) the
3365	  Grassmannian \(\mathrm{Gr}(r, d)\) is constructed by gluing
3366	  \(\binom{r}{d}\) affine charts \(U^I = \Spec\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) indexed by
3367	  size-\(d\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\). The chart \(U^I\)
3368	  carries the matrix \(X^I\) of size \(d \times r\) whose \(I\)-th \(d \times d\)
3369	  minor is the identity, with the remaining \(d(r-d)\) entries free
3370	  variables (so \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\)). The
3371	  transition maps \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I \cap U^J \to U^J \cap U^I\) defined
3372	  by \(X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) on the locus where \(\det X^I_J\) is
3373	  invertible satisfy the cocycle condition
3374	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\). The glued
3375	  \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec\mathbb{Z}\) is smooth of relative dimension
3376	  \(d(r-d)\). Separatedness is checked from the diagonal
3377	  \(\Delta_{I,J} \subset U^I \times U^J\) cut out by \(X^J_I X^I - X^J = 0\).
3378	  Properness follows from the valuative criterion for DVRs: given
3379	  \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over a DVR \(R\) with fraction
3380	  field \(K\), factoring through some chart \(U^I\) via a ring map
3381	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\), choose \(J\) minimising the valuation
3382	  \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) of the minor; on the chart \(U^J\) all entries have
3383	  non-negative valuation, so \(f\) extends uniquely to \(\Spec R\).
3384	
3385	  The universal quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\oplus r}
3386	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) is given on \(U^I\) by the matrix \(X^I\)
3387	  and glued by \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I \cap U^J)\). The
3388	  determinant line bundle \(\det\mathcal{U}\) has transition functions
3389	  \(\det g_{I,J} = 1/P^I_J\); the global sections
3390	  \(\sigma_I \in \Gamma(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), \det\mathcal{U})\) defined by
3391	  \(\sigma_I|_{U^J} = P^J_I\) form a base-point-free linear system separating
3392	  points over \(\Spec\mathbb{Z}\), giving an embedding into
3393	  \(\mathbb{P}^{\binom{r}{d}-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\), which is closed by
3394	  properness. Representability of \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d)\) is then verified
3395	  on the universal pair \((\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), u)\): a quotient
3396	  \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\oplus r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of
3397	  rank \(d\) on \(T\) is everywhere locally trivialised; on the locus where
3398	  the columns indexed by \(I\) are a basis the matrix of \(q\) has the form
3399	  \(X^I\), defining the classifying morphism \(T \to U^I \subset
3400	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Base change from \(\mathbb{Z}\) to a general noetherian
3401	  base \(S\) with a vector bundle \(V\) is direct: trivialise \(V\) on an open
3402	  cover and glue. For non-trivial \(V\) the same construction produces
3403	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) with Pl\"ucker embedding into
3404	  \(\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)\).
3405	\end{proof}
3406

1	\chapter{Regrouping isomorphism for the affine base-change tensor tower}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_RegroupHelper}
3	% This chapter blueprints the standalone, geometry-free helper that lives in
4	% AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/RegroupHelper.lean (1:1 slug). It is the pure
5	% tensor-algebra core of lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv in the FlatBaseChange
6	% chapter; it is split into its own compilation unit so the object-level
7	% ModuleCat iso can be obtained by a single LinearEquiv.toModuleIso application
8	% (the separate import normalises the `Module A (A ⊗[R] R')` instance diamond
9	% that blocks the same-file construction).
10	
11	\section{The R'-linear regrouping equivalence}
12	
13	\begin{lemma}
14	
15	\leanok
16	[Regrouping linear equivalence for the tensor tower]
17	  \label{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}
18	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}
19	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
20	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
21	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
22	  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
23	  % L933--938).
24	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
25	  % equality
26	  % $$
27	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
28	  % $$
29	  % as $R'$-modules."
30	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
31	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
32	  Let \(\psi : R \to R'\) and \(\varphi : R \to A\) be ring homomorphisms and \(M\)
33	  an \(A\)-module. On the \emph{canonical} Mathlib instances (with \(R'\) acting on
34	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) through the \(\texttt{Algebra.TensorProduct.rightAlgebra}\)
35	  factor, i.e.\ \(r' \cdot (a \otimes s) = a \otimes (r' s)\)) there is a bundled
36	  \(R'\)-linear isomorphism
37	  \[
38	    \operatorname{regroup} :
39	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M ,
40	  \]
41	  the ``equality as \(R'\)-modules'' of the affine base-change source. On generators
42	  it sends \((a \otimes r') \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes (a \cdot m)\); in
43	  particular \((1 \otimes r') \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes m\), with inverse
44	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\). It carries \emph{no} flatness
45	  hypothesis.
46	\end{lemma}
47	
48	\begin{proof}
49	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
50	  \leanok
51	  This is pure tensor algebra, and it is supplied \emph{directly} by Mathlib's
52	  pushout-form base-change cancellation
53	  \(\operatorname{Algebra.IsPushout.cancelBaseChange}\)
54	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}), instantiated at \(S = R'\),
55	  \(B = A \otimes_R R'\):
56	  \[
57	    \operatorname{cancelBaseChange} R\,R'\,A\,(A \otimes_R R')\,M :
58	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M .
59	  \]
60	  The required pushout instance \(\operatorname{Algebra.IsPushout} R\,R'\,A\,
61	  (A \otimes_R R')\) is supplied for free by \(\operatorname{TensorProduct.isPushout'}\).
62	  The decisive structural point is that this equivalence is \emph{natively}
63	  \(R'\)-linear: the cancellation is bundled as a \(\operatorname{LinearEquiv}\) over
64	  \(R'\) (its base-ring slot is \(S = R'\)), so the \(R'\)-linearity is part of the
65	  Mathlib datum with no hand-written scalar-compatibility obligation.
66	  On the generator it computes, by \(\texttt{cancelBaseChange\_tmul}\),
67	  \[
68	    (a \otimes r') \otimes m \;\longmapsto\; r' \otimes (a \cdot m) ,
69	  \]
70	  with inverse \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\)
71	  (\(\texttt{cancelBaseChange\_symm\_tmul}\)); the \(R'\)-action enters through the
72	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) factor (\(r' \cdot (a \otimes s) = a \otimes (r' s)\)). Being a
73	  single bundled Mathlib linear equivalence it is an \(R'\)-linear isomorphism, and it
74	  carries no flatness hypothesis.
75	
76	\end{proof}
77

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its core properties --- universal property and affine
26	base --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).

36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here \(S\) is a scheme, and \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra. This means that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a
49	  %  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}

79	\section{The relative-spectrum scheme}
80	\label{sec:relspec_scheme}
81	
82	The construction is given affine-locally: on every affine open \(U = \Spec R \subseteq X\) one
83	sets \(\pi^{-1}(U) := \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) regarded as an \(R\)-algebra, and the local pieces
84	glue compatibly because \(\mathcal{A}\) is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
85	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for an inclusion
86	\(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\) gives an open immersion of the corresponding Specs).

1	\chapter{Flattening Stratification of a Coherent Sheaf}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the classical Grothendieck/Nitsure~\S4
6	% \emph{generic flatness} theorem: over a noetherian integral base, a coherent
7	% sheaf on a finite-type \(X \to S\) becomes flat over a non-empty open
8	% subscheme of \(S\). Both its algebraic form (generic freeness of a finite
9	% module over a finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain) and its
10	% geometric form are recorded here as the prover-ready specification for
11	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}.
12	%
13	% This chapter is independently parallel-startable: it does not gate on the
14	% Route~A.1.a relative-Spec chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}.
15	
16	\section{Setup and motivation}
17	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
18	
19	Generic flatness is the foundational input to the flattening-stratification
20	construction underlying Grothendieck's existence theorem for the Quot scheme
21	(\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S4--\S5).  Its content is that flatness of a
22	coherent sheaf is a generic property over an integral base: over a noetherian
23	integral scheme \(S\), a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module on a finite-type morphism
24	\(X \to S\) becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open subscheme of
25	\(S\). Algebraically this is the statement that a finite module over a
26	finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain becomes free after inverting a
27	single non-zero element of the base ring.
28	
29	We work throughout with a noetherian (integral, where required) base scheme
30	\(S\), a finite-type morphism \(p : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
31	\(\F\).
32	
33	\section{Generic flatness}
34	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
35	
36	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
37	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
38	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
39	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
40	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
41	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
42	non-zero element of the base ring.
43	
44	\begin{theorem}
45	
46	
47	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
48	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
49	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatnessAlgebraic}
50	  % NOTE (iter-022): CLOSED, axiom-clean {propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}.
51	  % The Lean decl is single-universe `(A B M : Type u)` (was `Type*`): L5 and
52	  % `free_localizationAway_of_away_tower` are single-universe, and the dévissage
53	  % applies them to `C = B ⧸ 𝔭`, forcing A, B into one universe. Faithful for the
54	  % geometric use (scheme patches are single-universe).
55	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_base, lem:noeth_prime_filtration, lem:gf_splice_shortExact,
56	        lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators, lem:gf_polynomial_core, lem:gf_finite_module}
57	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
58	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
59	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
60	  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
61	  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.
62	  %  Then there exists an \(f\in A\), \(f\ne 0\), such that
63	  %  the localisation \(M_f\) is a free module over \(A_f\)."
64	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''.}
65	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite
66	  \(B\)-module. Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that the localisation
67	  \(M_f\) is a free \(A_f\)-module.
68	
69	\end{theorem}
70	
71	% INTENDED LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold pass creates the decl pinned above with
72	% exactly this signature):
73	%   (A B M : Type u) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]   % single-universe (iter-022)
74	%     [CommRing B] [Algebra A B] [Algebra.FiniteType A B]
75	%     [AddCommGroup M] [Module B M] [Module.Finite B M]
76	%     [Module A M] [IsScalarTower A B M] :
77	%     ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧
78	%       Module.Free (Localization.Away f) (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M)
79	
80	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Over the quotient field \(K\) of \(A\), the \(K\)-algebra
81	% \(B_K = K\otimes _AB\) is of finite type, and \(M_K = K\otimes_AM\) is a
82	% finite module over \(B_K\). Let \(n\) be the dimension of the support
83	% of \(M_K\) over \(\spec B_K\). We argue by induction on \(n\), starting
84	% with \(n=-1\) which is the case when \(M_K =0\). [...]
85	% Now let \(n\ge 0\), and let the lemma be proved for smaller values.
86	% As \(B\) is noetherian and \(M\) is assumed to be a finite \(B\)-module,
87	% there exists a finite filtration
88	% \(0=M_0 \subset \ldots  \subset M_r =M\)
89	% where each \(M_i\) is a \(B\)-submodule of \(M\) such that for each \(i\ge 1\)
90	% the quotient module \(M_i/M_{i-1}\) is isomorphic to \(B/\pp_i\) for
91	% some prime ideal \(\pp_i\) in \(B\). [...] By Noether normalisation lemma,
92	% there exist elements \(b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B\), such that \(K\otimes_AB\) is
93	% finite over its subalgebra \(K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]\) [...]"
94	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1772; full
95	% proof omitted for length, structural steps restated below in project notation.)
96	\begin{proof}
97	
98	  \textbf{Primary route — the finite \(A\)-module case.}
99	  Suppose first that \(M\) is module-finite over \(A\) itself. Since \(A\) is noetherian,
100	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). The generic fibre \(M_K = K \otimes_A M\)
